summary: Heavily obfuscated bulk-email/SMS toolkit with phishing-adjacent features
details: Package self-describes as an 'Advanced email sender' with 'red-team' and 'smtp' keywords (package.json). The CLI (entry at.ad/x0.js) exposes mass `send`, OAuth-based mailbox `contacts` extraction, DKIM key generation, carrier email-to-SMS gateway abuse, an open-redirect scanner (`web-redirect`), and a hidden web panel. The entire runtime under `.ad/` (45 files: x0.js–xz.js plus phone-validator.js, sms-providers.js, smtp-health-cache.js, web-command.js, from-generator.js) is shipped only in javascript-obfuscator form with RC4 string-array, self-defending and debug-protection traps, defeating casual audit. A license-activation path in x4.js posts a HWID (hash of /etc/machine-id, MAC, hostname, platform) to `http://api.nolimitent.xyz:4100/api/activate` over plain HTTP on `nolimit activate` and every 6h re-verify; this fires only when the user invokes the CLI, not at install. The `postinstall` hook only creates a Windows `.cmd` shim pointing at the package's own bin and performs no network I/O. From an installer-supply-chain standpoint, the code does not exfiltrate installer secrets, fetch attacker-controlled remote payloads, drop persistence, or establish backdoors against the installer's machine — installing or requiring the package does not harm the installer. The harms are (a) abuse directed at third parties when the installer actively operates the tool, and (b) heavy obfuscation that prevents the installer from verifying what the tool will do. Routing to human review: this is dual-use phishing-toolkit infrastructure with audit-defeating packaging, and the registry-policy decision (allow as a red-team utility vs. remove as abuse-enablement) is a judgment call outside the installer-harm threat model.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
